title: scenario_academically_absent
description: A student appears present but is mentally disengaged—phone use, sleeping, staring blankly.

timeline.title: Disengagement Observed
timeline.description: Teacher notices a student appears physically present but mentally absent.

timeline.title: Pattern Confirmed
timeline.description: Behavior is observed on multiple consecutive days—phone use, sleeping, blank staring.

timeline.title: Gentle Intervention
timeline.description: Teacher attempts to re-engage the student without public embarrassment.

timeline.title: Root Cause Exploration
timeline.description: Private conversation reveals potential underlying causes of disengagement.

timeline.title: Support Connection
timeline.description: Teacher connects the student to appropriate resources.

timeline.title: Resolved
timeline.description: Student re-engages with supports in place and teacher adjusts instruction.

node-1.title: Student Is Physically Present but Mentally Absent
node-1.situation: Tyler, a Grade 10 student, has been sitting in your class for the past three days using his phone under the desk, occasionally nodding off, and staring at the wall. He has not completed any class work this week. He is not causing disruption to others, but he is clearly not learning. You have not yet spoken to him about it.
node-1.question: How do you approach Tyler's disengagement?

options.label: A
options.text: During a transition or independent work period, approach Tyler quietly and privately say: 'Hey Tyler, I've noticed you seem somewhere else this week—I just want to check in, no pressure.' Avoid calling him out in front of peers.

options.label: B
options.text: Call Tyler out in front of the class, confiscate his phone, and tell him his sleeping and phone use are disrespectful and will result in a referral.

options.label: C
options.text: Leave Tyler alone since he is not disrupting anyone—his grades will reflect his choices at the end of the term.

node-2.title: Private Conversation Reveals Concerns
node-2.situation: Tyler opens up slightly during your quiet check-in. He mentions he has been working late shifts at a part-time job to help his family and is exhausted. He says school 'feels pointless right now.' He is not in crisis but is clearly struggling with external pressures and motivation. You want to keep the conversation productive.
node-2.question: How do you respond to Tyler's disclosure?

options.label: A
options.text: Validate Tyler's situation without minimizing it: 'That sounds really hard—balancing work and school is tough. I want to help make sure you can still succeed here. Let's figure out together what would make it more manageable.' Explore flexible supports and involve the counselor if appropriate.

options.label: B
options.text: Tell Tyler that school must come first, that his work situation is a personal choice, and that he needs to figure out his own time management.

options.label: C
options.text: Immediately report Tyler's employment situation to administration as a child welfare concern without speaking further with Tyler first.

node-3.title: Creating a Re-Engagement Plan
node-3.situation: Tyler agrees to meet with the school counselor and is open to some flexibility in how he completes work. You want to adjust your approach to re-engage him while maintaining academic standards. You also want to reflect on whether your instructional delivery is contributing to low engagement more broadly.
node-3.question: What instructional and support adjustments do you make?

options.label: A
options.text: Provide Tyler with a brief flexible completion plan for missed work with realistic deadlines. Refer him to the counselor for ongoing support. Reflect on your own instructional practice to increase relevance and student voice in future lessons to address broader disengagement.

options.label: B
options.text: Give Tyler a zero for all missed work—accommodating him would be unfair to students who completed their work on time.

options.label: C
options.text: Seat Tyler at the front of the class as a monitoring strategy and send daily emails to his parents without his knowledge.

node-4.title: Scenario Complete
node-4.situation: You recognized that academic absence is often a symptom of external stress, not defiance. Rather than punishing Tyler or ignoring the problem, you opened a private, compassionate conversation, connected him to the counselor, provided a realistic path to catch up, and reflected on your own practice. This approach addresses the root causes of disengagement and demonstrates that educators play a key role in student wellbeing alongside academics.
node-4.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
